scene_title: Court before the Duke of Albany's palace

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Go you before to Gloucester with these letters.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Acquaint my daughter no further with any thing you

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: know than comes from her demand out of the letter.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: If your diligence be not speedy, I shall be there afore you.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: I will not sleep, my lord, till I have delivered your letter.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: If a man’s brains were in’s heels, were’t not in danger of kibes?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ay, boy.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Then, I prithee, be merry; thy wit shall ne’er go slip-shod.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Ha, ha, ha!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Shalt see thy other daughter will use thee kindly; for though she’s

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: as like this as a crab’s like an apple, yet I can

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: tell what I can tell.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why, what canst thou tell, my boy?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: She will taste as like this as a crab does to a

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: crab. Thou canst tell why one’s nose stands i’ the middle on’s

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: face?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Why, to keep one’s eyes of either side’s nose; that what a

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: man cannot smell out, he may spy into.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I did her wrong—

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Canst tell how an oyster makes his shell?

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Nor I neither; but I can tell why a snail has a house.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Why?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Why, to put his head in; not to give it away to

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: his daughters, and leave his horns without a case.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: I will forget my nature. So kind a father! Be my horses ready?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Thy asses are gone about ’em. The reason why the seven stars

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: are no more than seven is a pretty reason.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Because they are not eight?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Yes, indeed: thou wouldst make a good fool.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: To take ’t again perforce! Monster ingratitude!

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: If thou wert my fool, nuncle, I’ld have thee beaten for being

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: old before thy time.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: How’s that?

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Thou shouldst not have been old till thou hadst been wise.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: O, let me not be mad, not mad, sweet heaven!

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Keep me in temper: I would not be mad!

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Ready, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING LEAR
lines.text: Come, boy.

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: She that’s a maid now, and laughs at my departure,

lines.speaker: FOOL
lines.text: Shall not be a maid long, unless things be cut shorter.

stage_directions.text: Enter King Lear, Kent, and Fool.
How now! are the horses ready?

glosses.definition: Lear's first explicit fear of losing his reason
keep me sane; preserve my mental balance
any question Regan might ask based on my letter
the Fool's riddle — the snail keeps its shell; Lear gave his away
the Pleiades — the Fool's riddle (why seven? because they are not eight)
the Fool's final parting jest — a maid who laughs at Lear's departure won't be a maid long

annotations.title: 'I would not be mad' — Lear names the fear
annotations.body: In six lines at the end of 1.5 Lear says aloud what he has been avoiding: 'O, let me not be mad, not mad, sweet heaven! / Keep me in temper: I would not be mad.' This is the first explicit admission. Before this he has raged; now he has cognitive insight into his own trajectory. The self-awareness is not a protection — he goes mad anyway — but it is the beginning of the play's central consciousness, the king watching himself come apart. Every later madness has this moment as its root: Lear knew, and the knowing could not save him.

trials.prompt: What does Lear recognize at the end of 1.5?
options: That he has made a mistake with his retinue
That he fears losing his reason — 'let me not be mad, not mad, sweet heaven!' — the first explicit acknowledgment of the trajectory toward madness
That he wishes to see Cordelia
